title: Assault on Doomrock - An Underrated Gem
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dgkZoy66esM

Daniel (@dungeondive) makes the case for Assault on Doomrock — here the second printing with the Doompocalypse expansion — as one of the most unique and underrated games he owns, and one he rarely plays because it stresses him out. Unlike most thematic Ameritrash crawls that trade tight decisions for experience, Doomrock is almost pure choice: a brutally tight economy of actions where a single misstep can cost you the game. It splits into an adventure phase — a time-budgeted race through a deck of areas, spending precious 'time' to explore, loot, level and camp before reaching Doomrock — and a highly abstract battle phase where positioning is about adjacency and groups rather than a grid. Combat is Yahtzee-like dice allocation: three rolls, then assign dice to skill cards, with the skills themselves supplying your stat line. He praises the exposure and peril systems, the terrain, and the sheer density of items and skills, and shares the famous story of the designer accidentally using the ASL 'I love you' sign instead of devil horns for heroism. The catch: three-hour playtimes and real analysis paralysis. Recommended with the expansion for anyone craving decisions over spectacle.

----

Doomrock is the rare thematic game that lives and dies on hard choices rather than the experience — where do you land when a crawl asks you to think this hard? Refreshing, or exhausting?
